content.text: Debugging is the process of locating the root cause of a defect and making a targeted fix. In Angular, most runtime bugs fall into a small number of categories: change-detection not propagating, HTTP errors, null-reference exceptions, routing misconfiguration, and performance regressions. Matching the symptom to the right tool is the fastest path to a fix.

attrs.alt: Angular debugging strategy: symptom → primary tool → common fix

content.text: The matrix above maps the seven most common Angular symptoms to the DevTools panel that surfaces the evidence and to the code-level fix that resolves it. The sections below walk through each workflow in detail.

Before reaching for DevTools, read the full error in the browser console. Angular error messages include the component class name, the template file, and the line number that triggered the exception. Three common patterns:
This error means a binding value changed
Angular ran change detection in the same tick. It only appears in development mode and is Angular's way of telling you that your component has side effects in its check cycle.

ERROR: ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenCheckedError:
Expression has changed after it was checked.
Previous value: 'ngIf: false'. Current value: 'ngIf: true'.
  at ProductListComponent.ngAfterViewInit (product-list.component.ts:45)
without telling Angular to re-run detection.
so it runs in the next microtask, after the current detection cycle completes.
Thrown when Angular cannot find a provider for a service in the injector hierarchy.
ERROR: NullInjectorError: No provider for ProductService!
array of the module or component that uses it — or decorate the service with
If the service is in a lazy-loaded module, it must be provided inside that module or in root — it cannot be injected into a component that loads before the module.
Angular 14+ prefixes runtime errors with NG codes. Look up the exact code on angular.dev/errors for a description and fix examples. These codes appear in the console alongside the full stack trace.

The most common class of Angular bugs is a template binding that does not update when the underlying data changes. The root cause is almost always one of two things: the component uses OnPush and Angular skips it because no input reference changed, or a mutable array/object was modified in place without creating a new reference.
In Elements, click the component element that is not updating (e.g.
If the property shows the expected data, Angular has the correct value but has not re-rendered.
. If the template updates, change detection was not running.
Fixing Mutable State with New References
Angular's HttpClient is built on RxJS Observables. Errors from the server arrive as error notifications on the Observable stream, not as thrown exceptions.
Inspecting Requests in the Network Panel
Trigger the Angular action that should make the request.
Click the request row. Check the Status (4xx/5xx = server error), Headers (are auth headers present?), and the Preview tab (parsed error body).
Catching and Logging Errors in the Service
Using an HttpInterceptor to Log All Requests
Source maps let the browser translate the compiled JavaScript back to your TypeScript source files. With source maps enabled, you can set breakpoints directly in .ts files in the Sources panel.
Source maps are enabled by default for
Right-click a line number in the Sources panel and choose
. The debugger only pauses when the expression is truthy, reducing the number of times you have to step through a loop:
. Instead of pausing execution, DevTools logs an expression to the Console each time the line executes. Use this to trace values in hot paths (
) without flooding the console with manual
calls that you might forget to remove:

While paused at a breakpoint, the Watch panel on the right lets you add arbitrary JavaScript expressions that are re-evaluated every time the debugger pauses. Add expressions like this.items.length or this.user?.id to monitor them across multiple breakpoint hits without typing them in the Console each time.
in a template means a binding was evaluated before the data was available. The safest fix is the async pipe:

{{ user.name }}

{{ (user$ | async)?.name }}

@if (user) {

{{ user.name }}

}

{{ user.name }}

Template errors display in the Problems panel in VS Code (with Angular Language Service) and in the browser console at runtime. The error includes the file name and the expression that failed. Pay attention to errors like:
Property 'x' does not exist on type 'Y'
Typo in property name or missing @Input()
Fix the property name; add the @Input decorator
Method signature mismatch between TS and template
Update method signature or template call
Pipe not imported in the module or standalone component
Routing bugs — wrong component rendered, redirect loop, guard never resolving — can be diagnosed by enabling the router's debug trace:

With debug tracing on, every router event (NavigationStart, GuardsCheckStart, ActivationEnd, NavigationEnd, NavigationCancel) is logged to the console with its full payload. NavigationCancel entries include the reason code that explains why navigation was aborted.
Inspecting Active Route in the Console
Angular CLI uses Karma + Jasmine for unit tests by default. When
reports a failing spec, you can open the Karma debug page and set breakpoints in the compiled test file.
. A Chrome window opens showing Karma's test runner.
(top-right of the Karma window) to open a debuggable page at
Open DevTools in that window (F12) and navigate to Sources.
Find your test file under the webpack:// tree and set a breakpoint inside the failing spec.
Press F5 to reload the debug page — Karma re-runs the tests and the breakpoint fires.
Isolating a Single Test with fdescribe / fit
Remove all f-prefixes (fdescribe, fit) before committing. Leaving them in means only the focused tests run in CI, causing the rest to be silently skipped.
or a dev build for all debugging sessions
Noise in production logs; may expose sensitive data
Parent and child components share the same reference; hard to trace
Treat @Input as immutable; clone before modifying
Pauses every user's browser in production
Watching derived state, not root cause
Fix the symptom without finding the source
Follow the data flow backwards from the template to the service to the API
RxJS pipelines are composable but hard to trace when values are not flowing as expected. The recommended technique is to insert a tap operator for visibility without changing the stream's behaviour.
operators before committing — they are debugging helpers, not production instrumentation. If you need ongoing observability, use a logging interceptor or a structured logging library instead.
If a template shows the old value even though the Observable emitted new data, the common causes are:
was not used — the component subscribed manually but forgot to call
the last emit — only the buffered value arrives, and it may be stale.

Angular Reactive Forms expose their full validation state through the FormGroup and FormControl APIs, which you can inspect directly from the component.
Inspecting Form State in the Template (Development)

{{ form.value | json }}
{{ form.errors | json }}
Valid: {{ form.valid }}, Dirty: {{ form.dirty }}, Touched: {{ form.touched }}
Inspecting Form State from the Console
Submit enabled when field is invalid
is null but submit should be blocked
Add cross-field validators to the FormGroup, not individual controls
Ensure async validator completes — check it emits or resolves
By default, ng build --configuration production strips source maps to reduce bundle size and prevent exposing source code. For debugging production issues there are two strategies:
in angular.json. This generates .js.map files but does not link them from the bundle (no sourceMappingURL comment), so end-users cannot access them. You can still load them locally in DevTools:
Copy the .map files from the dist folder to the same location locally.
In DevTools Sources panel, right-click a script file and choose
to manually point to the .map file.
For persistent production error monitoring, integrate an error tracking service. Angular provides the ErrorHandler class for intercepting uncaught errors:
Angular Universal (SSR) renders components on the Node.js server before sending HTML to the browser. Bugs that appear only in SSR (but not in
) are usually caused by code that references browser-only APIs:
directly — these do not exist in Node.js.
to check whether the platform is browser.
DOM manipulation in lifecycle hooks that run on the server (ngOnInit, ngAfterViewInit).
(the command generated by Angular Universal's schematic). Node.js errors appear in the terminal, not the browser console.

Effective Angular debugging starts with reading the full error message and identifying the symptom category. Use the Console ng.* helpers and Angular DevTools for component-state and change-detection bugs; the Network panel for HTTP issues; Sources breakpoints for logic errors in TypeScript; and Karma's debug page for unit test failures. Resist the urge to sprinkle console.log everywhere — targeted breakpoints, logpoints, and the ng.getComponent API give cleaner evidence with less cleanup.
